title: Μῦς καὶ βάτραχος
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 244 (= Perry 384). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Χερσαῖος μῦς κακῇ μοίρᾳ βατράχῳ ἐφιλιώθη.
sentences.tr: Chersaios mys kakēi moirāi batrachōi ephiliōthē.
sentences.l: A land mouse, by-ill fate, with-a-frog became-friends.
sentences.i: A land mouse, in an evil hour, struck up a friendship with a frog.
sentences.n: κακῇ μοίρᾳ — 'by bad fate': the narrator dooms the friendship in the first line. ἐφιλιώθη picks up the same φιλ- root as the Country Mouse's ἐφίλει: these mouse fables open on friendship and test it.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ βάτραχος κακῶς βουλευσάμενος τὸν πόδα τοῦ μυὸς τῷ ἑαυτοῦ ποδὶ συνέδησε.
sentences.tr: Ho de batrachos kakōs bouleusamenos ton poda tou myos tōi heautou podi synedēse.
sentences.l: And the frog, ill having-counselled, the foot of-the mouse to-the of-himself foot bound-together.
sentences.i: The frog, with treachery in mind, tied the mouse's foot to his own.

sentences.t: Καὶ πρῶτον μὲν ἐπὶ τῆς χώρας ἦλθον σῖτον δειπνήσοντες· ἔπειτα δὲ τῷ χείλει τῆς λίμνης πλησιάσαντες, ὁ μὲν βάτραχος τὸν μῦν εἰς τὸ βάθος κατῆγεν, αὐτὸς βρυάζων τῷ ὕδατι καὶ τὸ βρεκεκεκὲξ ἀνακράζων.
sentences.tr: Kai prōton men epi tēs chōras ēlthon siton deipnēsontes: epeita de tōi cheilei tēs limnēs plēsiasantes, ho men batrachos ton myn eis to bathos katēgen, autos bryazōn tōi hydati kai to brekekekex anakrazōn.
sentences.l: And first on the land they-went, grain about-to-dine-on: but then, to-the lip of-the pond having-drawn-near, the frog the mouse into the depth dragged-down, himself revelling in-the water and the brekekekex croaking-out.
sentences.i: At first they went about on land, dining on grain; but when they came to the edge of the pond, the frog dragged the mouse down into the deep, revelling in the water and croaking his brekekekex.
sentences.n: βρεκεκεκέξ is the frog's croak spelled out — the same onomatopoeia Aristophanes made a chorus of in the Frogs (βρεκεκεκὲξ κοὰξ κοάξ). τὸ χεῖλος, 'lip,' serves for the edge of a pond as in English.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ ἄθλιος μῦς τῷ ὕδατι φυσηθεὶς ἐτεθνήκει· ἐπέπλει δὲ τῷ ποδὶ τοῦ βατράχου συνδεδεμένος.
sentences.tr: Ho de athlios mys tōi hydati physētheis etethnēkei: epeplei de tōi podi tou batrachou syndedemenos.
sentences.l: And the wretched mouse, with-the water having-been-bloated, was-dead: and he-floated, to-the foot of-the frog bound-together.
sentences.i: The poor mouse, bloated with water, was dead — but he floated, still tied to the frog's foot.
sentences.n: ἐτεθνήκει, pluperfect: 'was (already) dead' — the story steps past the death to its consequence. The rope that killed the mouse is about to become the frog's own tether.

sentences.t: Ἰκτῖνος δὲ τοῦτον ἰδὼν τοῖς ὄνυξιν ἥρπασεν· βάτραχος δὲ δεσμώτης ἐπηκολούθει, δεῖπνον καὶ αὐτὸς τῷ ἰκτίνῳ γενόμενος.
sentences.tr: Iktinos de touton idōn tois onyxin hērpasen: batrachos de desmōtēs epēkolouthei, deipnon kai autos tōi iktinōi genomenos.
sentences.l: And a kite, him having-seen, with-the talons snatched-him: and the frog, a prisoner, followed-after, dinner also himself for-the kite having-become.
sentences.i: A kite spotted the floating mouse and snatched it up in his talons — and the frog followed, in chains, becoming the kite's dinner too.
sentences.n: δεσμώτης, 'prisoner' — the frog's own knot has changed sides. δεῖπνον καὶ αὐτός: the word that began as their shared meal (δειπνήσοντες) returns with the diners on the menu.

sentences.t: Ὅτι, κἂν νεκρὸς ᾖ τις, ἰσχύει πρὸς ἄμυναν· ἡ γὰρ θεία δίκη ἐφορᾷ πάντα καὶ τὸ ἴσον ἀποδίδωσι ζυγοστατοῦσα.
sentences.tr: Hoti, kan nekros ēi tis, ischyei pros amynan: hē gar theia dikē ephorāi panta kai to ison apodidōsi zygostatousa.
sentences.l: That, even-if dead one be, one-has-force for requital: for the divine justice watches-over all-things and the equal renders, weighing-in-the-balance.
sentences.i: Even the dead can be avenged: divine justice watches over everything and, holding the balance, renders measure for measure.
sentences.n: ζυγοστατοῦσα — 'standing at the scales': Justice literally weighs. This moral's θεία δίκη is a rarer, more solemn register than the usual worldly advice; the note of heaven fits a fable where the killer is punished by a bird from the sky.
